hook: Dominated handhelds, then smartphones beat webOS
fate: Acquired by HP in 2010 for 1.2 billion dollars. webOS hardware was discontinued in 2011. The brand trademark was later sold to TCL Corporation in 2014 and the webOS platform was acquired by LG Electronics in 2013 for use in smart televisions.
summary: Pioneer of handheld PDAs and early smartphones including the Pilot, Treo, and Pre. Acquired by HP in 2010 for 1.2 billion dollars. webOS devices were discontinued in 2011 after the HP TouchPad failed in the market.
overview_md:
Palm Computing was founded in 1992 by Jeff Hawkins, Donna Dubinsky, and Ed Colligan with one goal in mind. They wanted to build a pocket-sized computer that people would actually use every day. Earlier attempts by Apple and others had resulted in devices that were too heavy, too slow, and too expensive. Palm took a different approach by stripping the concept down to what mattered most.

The result was the PalmPilot, launched in 1996 at a price of 299 dollars. It was a grayscale, stylus-driven organizer that weighed just 5.7 ounces and fit in a shirt pocket. The Graffiti handwriting system let users write letters in a simplified alphabet that the device could reliably recognize. HotSync connected the device to a desktop computer in seconds, keeping contacts, calendars, and notes perfectly aligned between both machines. Over one million PalmPilot units sold in the first 18 months. Palm captured more than 70 percent of the PDA market within a few years. For a while, the word PalmPilot functioned as a synonym for the entire category the way Xerox had for photocopying.

Palm changed hands several times during its rise. U.S. Robotics acquired the company in 1995 for 44 million dollars. 3Com then acquired U.S. Robotics in 1997 and absorbed Palm along with it. The founders grew frustrated with 3Com's priorities and left in 1998 to start Handspring, a competing company that built Palm OS devices of its own including the Visor line. 3Com spun Palm off as an independent public company in March 2000. On its first day of trading during the dot-com bubble, Palm shares hit 95 dollars. A year later they had lost 90 percent of their value.

In 2003 Palm reacquired Handspring and inherited the Treo smartphone line. The Treo 600 was one of the first devices to put a full keyboard, a phone, and a capable mobile browser into a single pocketable form factor. Business users adopted it widely. It competed directly with the BlackBerry for the professional market and won converts who appreciated its touchscreen alongside the physical keyboard. For several years the Treo line kept Palm commercially relevant even as the broader PDA category began to shrink.

The arrival of the iPhone in 2007 reset every assumption about what a smartphone should be. Android followed in 2008. Palm's existing hardware and the aging Palm OS could not match the new standard being set. The company needed a complete reinvention.

Jon Rubinstein, who had previously led hardware engineering at Apple and was credited with designing the original iPod, joined Palm as executive chairman in 2007 and became CEO in 2009. He oversaw the development of webOS from the ground up. WebOS launched with the Palm Pre in June 2009 exclusively on Sprint. The operating system introduced card-based multitasking that let users flip between open applications and flick them away to close them. Synergy pulled together email, contacts, and calendars from multiple accounts including Gmail, Facebook, and Exchange into unified views without requiring manual setup. Notifications appeared at the bottom of the screen without interrupting whatever the user was doing. Many of these patterns were later adopted by iOS and Android in subsequent years.

Critics responded warmly to the Pre and webOS. The hardware received some complaints about build quality and a sharp edge along the sliding mechanism. The deeper problem was scale. Sprint was a smaller carrier than AT&T or Verizon, limiting Palm's potential audience. The App Catalog launched with 18 apps and grew to around 1,000 by early 2010, far behind the tens of thousands available on iPhone and Android. Palm could not attract enough developers to close the gap quickly enough.

By early 2010 Palm was burning through cash. The company reported a quarterly loss of 22 million dollars in March 2010 and warned that revenue would fall well short of earlier guidance. Its stock, which had recovered to around 17 dollars on the launch of the Pre, fell back toward 3 dollars. Speculation about a sale became public.

HP announced on April 28 2010 that it would acquire Palm for 1.2 billion dollars at 5.70 per share in an all-cash deal. The acquisition closed on July 1 2010. HP's stated plan was to put webOS on every device it made, including smartphones, tablets, printers, and computers. In February 2011 HP unveiled the Pre 3, the Veer, and the HP TouchPad tablet. None of them were branded as Palm products. The Palm name was quietly retired when HP renamed the division the webOS Global Business Unit in July 2011.

The TouchPad launched in July 2011 at 499 dollars, matching the iPad's entry price. Sales were disastrous. HP announced on August 18 2011 that it was immediately ending production of all webOS hardware. The remaining TouchPad inventory was cleared through a fire sale at 99 dollars, which ironically produced a massive spike in demand that HP could no longer supply. CEO Leo Apotheker's decision to kill webOS hardware was one of several moves that caused HP's share price to fall 45 percent. Apotheker was replaced by Meg Whitman later that year.

The pieces of Palm were eventually distributed across several companies. HP sold the webOS platform to LG Electronics in February 2013 for use in its smart television lineup, where it remains in use today. HP sold approximately 1,400 Palm patents to Qualcomm in 2014. The Palm trademark itself was sold in October 2014 to a shelf company controlled by a regional president of TCL Corporation, the Chinese electronics conglomerate that markets phones under the Alcatel brand. TCL announced in January 2015 that it planned to bring Palm back as an Android smartphone brand built around a Silicon Valley team. A small Palm-branded companion phone appeared in 2018 but found no meaningful audience.

Palm's real legacy is not in any product that followed but in what its engineers figured out years before anyone else. The PalmPilot proved the market for a third category of computing device that sat between a phone and a laptop. The Treo proved that a smartphone with a physical keyboard and a touchscreen could replace a laptop for road workers. WebOS proved that a mobile operating system could be built around multitasking, cloud accounts, and frictionless notifications in a way that later became the industry standard. The company that invented those ideas did not survive long enough to profit from them, but the ideas are everywhere.

timeline.text: Jeff Hawkins, Donna Dubinsky, and Ed Colligan found Palm Computing to build a practical pocket organizer.
U.S. Robotics acquires Palm for 44 million dollars.
The PalmPilot 1000 launches at 299 dollars. Over one million units sell in the first 18 months.
3Com acquires U.S. Robotics and Palm becomes a 3Com subsidiary.
Founders Hawkins, Dubinsky, and Colligan leave to start Handspring after disagreements with 3Com.
3Com spins Palm off as an independent public company. Shares hit 95 dollars on the first day of trading.
Palm reacquires Handspring, bringing the Treo smartphone line in house.
The Palm Pre launches on Sprint running webOS, introducing card multitasking and Synergy.
HP completes its acquisition of Palm for 1.2 billion dollars at 5.70 per share.
HP ends all webOS hardware production. The Palm brand is effectively retired.
HP sells webOS to LG Electronics for use in smart televisions.
HP sells the Palm trademark to a TCL-linked entity. Qualcomm separately acquires 1,400 Palm patents from HP.

sources.label: HP Investor Relations – HP to Acquire Palm for $1.2 Billion (April 2010)
HP Investor Relations – HP Completes Palm Acquisition (July 2010)
PCWorld – A Brief History of Palm
